segments.text: Nice bright day in College Station.
Have the pleasure of chatting with Harrison
Hurth, fellow Presbyterian, and a dear friend about his experiences here at
This is February 15, 1999, a bright sunny day.
honor of chatting with Harrison Hurth.
Before we start talking about A&M so
that anyone in the future might know a little bit about your background before
A&M, give us a brief biographical summary of your experience before coming
Okay, well I was born in Pontiac, Illinois, I went to undergraduate school at Illinois Western University.
I went then to the University of Illinois for a master's degree, and I taught for a number of years in Illinois high school.
And I came to A&M when the University of Illinois placement office sent me a notice that A&M wanted some teachers for summer school.
That didn't sound like a summer resort, and I thought, well, that's a chance to give the collegiate level a shot, so I applied.
That was 19, and Dr. Thomas Mayo replied and said he didn't need anyone after all, but would be contacting me in the fall,
Something between a possibility and a polite brush off.
And I think on the Fourth of July weekend, I re-enlisted, and he had called me prior to that.
And I talked it over with him, and he did make, he had made me an offer.
So I called on that weekend, so they'd come a week late in 19, the 4th, September 19th.
My younger daughter had just been, was not really doing too well.
Margaret was still in the hospital.
I came down alone, and someone had been covering for me.
I'd be interested, you came by car?
And what, how much gear did you bring in that car?
Well, I had a, what I had was a, I didn't have a very big truck, so I bought enough stuff to survive,
because I had enough clothing to last until Thanksgiving.
And when I got my wife, I met her in Memphis.
And then you all drove back.
Yeah, what happened this time, we sent her mother to Memphis.
I went to Memphis on the train, and we drove back there in her mother's car.
Where were you all living then?
Well, we lived out to A&M Annex.
The University of New College had taken over the old Bryan Air Base, and turned it into the A&M Annex.
And we had an apartment in what had been an officer's ward in the hospital.
I was going to bring a new baby down.
I spent every evening, old Dutch cleanser, scrubbing down the wall.
And we had a large room big enough for us.
We did not use the apartment behind it.
Five radiators, two or three commodes.
What kind of furniture was in it?
Well, no I'm sorry, it was not furnished.
When the furniture truck drove up, the driver demanded $700, which I had a lot of money then.
I hot-footed it into the First National Bank.
And I've banked with them ever since.
I can imagine how that would be important.
Air Base experience, I think, is very, very important, that story.
About what was the enrollment out there, and the faculty.
1946 was the year of the veterans.
The enrollment of the college was virtually double the total enrollment.
It must have been in the area of seven classes.
And they put a freshman class.
And some of those were veterans and some were not.
I'm not sure whether veterans went to the annex or not.
All right, so you're implying that most of them were right out of high school.
Most of them were freshmen right out of high school.
They had Harrington and John Paul Abbott at different times, whatever the title was.
I believe they called them deans and directed the operation.
And you offered the full range of courses for freshmen.
Did any of them come into the main campus to take any courses?
Faculty people, I taught mostly on the main campus, actually.
Those of us who did carpool.
I had a semester or two when I taught a couple of days at the annex.
Describe the students and their capability.
Well, I dealt largely with them.
But I dealt very largely with them in a sense of purpose and what they were there for.
Do you remember any anecdotes or examples of that purpose?
Purpose and determination on their part?
Any of them ever say anything or do anything that illustrated that?
I would have thought, you know, that they had all the military they wanted.
Were there instances when you could sense that the experience of where they'd been
and what they'd seen helped them to understand something that you were trying to teach them?
Well, obviously we knew they were there.
About what was the enrollment per section?
And you gave them the same kind of assignments and homework that you would have any other classes?
Let's go back to the air base.
There were many hundreds of students out there and almost all of their activities were out there.
I know that's when the fish drill team was created and I understand there was a freshman band.
What interaction was there between the main campus of the students and the air base?
I think the sophomores were very unhappy that they weren't getting out the freshmen.
Was there any hazing out there?
There was hazing out there because there were only freshmen.
Now, about how many of you faculty lived out there?
It must have been 30 or 40 families.
There was a housing area near the entrance called The Village and a group lived up there.
The rest of us were in the Lee Martins and the Staley Crestwells.
sent an advance summoner and he said, I'll take this in.
So I had to dig up the other end.
Did many others garden out there?
I just wanted to be sure it was turning.
Was there any hazing of the freshmen?
But they were organized in military fashion.
Not many of them had cars.
They kidded me on one of the first meals we had.
I said, you know I don't like beans, but these are the best beans I ever ate.
So you all dined in the old
Did you faculty members eat in the
dining hall or did you eat on your own at home?
ate in the dining hall until I met most people.
Describe the faculty social life between
you faculty families that were out there.
there was an article in the paper that the bridge club out there really
had well get together as a group.
Yes, at different times, Dr. Harrington.
Now did that have, what was the official title of that
And you were out there how long?
I didn't know it was that long.
between World War II and Vietnam.
Well, but I'm assuming that was the base.
And you and your wife had two daughters at that point.
Was there an elementary school out there for those that were
She showed us her report card.
My A has it marked black.
Uh oh, I was gonna say.
She was there, I think, two years and then left to Bowie.
Alright, as you look back on it, how did those two years,
were those good years for her?
Alright, so you taught out there
And this, what's the address of this house?
Okay, describe this, the neighborhood when you all moved in.
Well, it really, it was pretty.
You know, the man in the extension thought she was going to lose her shirt.
So they formed a committee to try to, Miss Jenny went ahead and did
Then, I don't know much about this, but shortly
thereafter, they formed what they called the corporation.
And they built the good money houses.
am I correct that it was called Southside or Southgate?
east of us, just south of what used to be called Jersey?
The girls grow up here, your two daughters.
Um, and you then, did you teach a fourth year out at the air base or did you
start teaching full time here on the campus that
About how big was the English department
let's try to recall as many of those as you can.
Who were some of the senior English
Well, Dr. Mayo headed the department.
Mayo for someone that may never have met him.
Well, he's a very charming man.
Very lively and articulate and learned
Couldn't bear a moment of silence.
Now, he was the department head?
Yes, he had been moved over.
And y'all, the department office in the academic building?
Alright, they were on the, I think they're the
fourth or the fifth, whatever it is.
Yeah, so we were on the floor below them, whatever that was.
I remember later when I was working every night trying to write a
dissertation, my wife would worry about me and I'd say, go in there and load the office.
The architects were there all night.
I think the students called him Jelly Abbott.
He spent one year on the board.
I don't think he ever thought of it.
Did the students give him a nickname?
Described himself as a scholar with
maybe he said a foul manners.
other areas, I know there were lots
of Aggies, and you started working on your
what that trailer area was called.
with all the water in it
We went up with our younger daughter only,
We were on the right side of town.
They went to the best schools
Mary Margaret to West High School.
on the right side of town.
She worked in Wisconsin General Hospital
I was along with the kids.
The little girls would go down
and listen to the young ladies
We had no woman to go down
You knew you had a slot coming back.
Yes, I finished all of it.
How many pages did you write?
I said I didn't write any
What were the changes here at A&M?
The moment goes down a little
who was the head of Lamar.
did a concordance to a student
the students generalize about the students
I don't know how many majors.
taking one of your advanced courses
We had some who did that.
or incidents in classes where the students
Your nickname I understand was Happy.
I don't remember how I found it out.
Let's stay with these nicknames a little.
If so, I didn't know it.
Thompson and Dr. Dan the Grade Point Man.
What did those nicknames really mean?
Maybe affection may be too strong.
I think it was kind of affection and respect
Now, correct me if I'm wrong.
